title: US Senate to Vote This Week on Bill to Bar California 2035 EV Plan
content: The U.S. Senate is preparing to vote on legislation that would block Californiaâ€™s landmark rule to end the sale of gasoline-only vehicles by 2035. This action follows the Houseâ€™s earlier passage of a bill to repeal an Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) waiver granted to California in December under the Biden administration. The waiver allows the state to require that at least 80% of new vehicles sold by 2035 be electric. Californiaâ€™s zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) rule has already been adopted by 11 other states, accounting for nearly 40% of the U.S. auto market. Senate Majority Whip John Thune and other critics argue that because of Californiaâ€™s market size, the mandate has a de facto nationwide effect, forcing automakers to reduce the production of gasoline vehicles and invest heavily in electric models, even in states with lower EV demand. Automakers have expressed concern that the ZEV requirements are unrealistic given current market conditions and consumer demand. California, on the other hand, defends its authority under the Clean Air Act to set stricter emissions standards and argues that reducing emissions is essential to tackling climate change and improving air quality. Californiaâ€™s Air Resources Board has set interim goals, including a requirement that 35% of new light-duty vehicles in 2026 be zero-emission, increasing to 68% by 2030. The Biden administration has also backed aggressive federal fuel economy and emissions rules, which the Senate may seek to reverse.
author: Analysts Account
